Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.step_plot_static(time, state) `

Notes

Step plots are useful for piecewise-constant series.

Module contents

Bivariate visualization charts - static and interactive versions.

class dataviz.bivariate.BivariateStats(n: int, missing_x: int, missing_y: int, pearson: float, spearman: float, covariance: float, slope: float, intercept: float, r_squared: float, x_mean: float, y_mean: float, x_std: float, y_std: float)[source]

Bases: object

Summary statistics for a pair of variables.

Parameters:

  • n (int) – Number of observations used in the calculations.

  • missing_x (int) – Count of missing x values in the original input.

  • missing_y (int) – Count of missing y values in the original input.

  • pearson (float) – Pearson correlation coefficient.

  • spearman (float) – Spearman rank correlation coefficient.

  • covariance (float) – Sample covariance between x and y.

  • slope (float) – Least-squares linear slope.

  • intercept (float) – Least-squares linear intercept.

  • r_squared (float) – Coefficient of determination for the linear fit.

  • x_mean (float) – Mean of x.

  • y_mean (float) – Mean of y.

  • x_std (float) – Sample standard deviation of x.

  • y_std (float) – Sample standard deviation of y.

Returns:

Immutable bivariate summary object.

Return type:

BivariateStats

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be represented as numeric statistics.

  • ValueError – If insufficient observations are available.

Example

`python stats = dv.bivariate.bivariate_summary(x, y) `

Notes

Instances are returned by bivariate_summary and by plotting functions with return_stats=True.

covariance: float
intercept: float
missing_x: int
missing_y: int
n: int
pearson: float
r_squared: float
slope: float
spearman: float
x_mean: float
x_std: float
y_mean: float
y_std: float
dataviz.bivariate.area_between(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y_lower: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y_upper: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str = 'Area Between Curves', xlabel: str = 'X', ylabel: str = 'Y', color: str = 'steelblue', alpha: float = 0.3, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static filled area between two y-series.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y_lower (SeriesLike) – Lower boundary values.

  • y_upper (SeriesLike) – Upper boundary values.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • color (str) – Fill color.

  • alpha (float) – Fill opacity.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.fill_between.

Returns:

Filled area axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs are not numeric.

  • ValueError – If input lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.area_between_static(x, lower, upper) `

Notes

This chart is useful for bands, intervals, or envelopes.

dataviz.bivariate.area_between_interactive(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y_lower: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y_upper: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str = 'Area Between Curves', xlabel: str = 'X', ylabel: str = 'Y', color: str = 'rgba(70, 130, 180, 0.35)', line_color: str = 'steelblue', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive filled area between two y-series.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y_lower (SeriesLike) – Lower boundary values.

  • y_upper (SeriesLike) – Upper boundary values.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • color (str) – Fill color.

  • line_color (str) – Boundary line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly layout template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

Returns:

Interactive filled band figure.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs are not numeric.

  • ValueError – If input lengths differ.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.area_between_interactive(x, lower, upper) `

Notes

The upper trace fills to the lower trace using Plotly’s tonexty mode.

dataviz.bivariate.area_between_static(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y_lower: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y_upper: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str = 'Area Between Curves', xlabel: str = 'X', ylabel: str = 'Y', color: str = 'steelblue', alpha: float = 0.3, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static filled area between two y-series.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y_lower (SeriesLike) – Lower boundary values.

  • y_upper (SeriesLike) – Upper boundary values.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • xlabel (str) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (str) – Y-axis label.

  • color (str) – Fill color.

  • alpha (float) – Fill opacity.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.fill_between.

Returns:

Filled area axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs are not numeric.

  • ValueError – If input lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.area_between_static(x, lower, upper) `

Notes

This chart is useful for bands, intervals, or envelopes.

dataviz.bivariate.binned_mean_plot(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 10, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str = 'steelblue', marker: str = 'o', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static line chart of mean y values across x bins.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Numeric values used to create bins.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Numeric values averaged within each bin.

  • bins (int) – Number of x bins.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • color (str) – Line and marker color.

  • marker (str) – Marker style.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.plot.

Returns:

Binned mean plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y is not numeric.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.binned_mean_plot_static(age, income) `

Notes

Binned means help reveal nonlinear relationships in noisy scatter data.

dataviz.bivariate.binned_mean_plot_interactive(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 10, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str = 'steelblue', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive line chart of mean y values across x bins.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Numeric values used to create bins.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Numeric values averaged within each bin.

  • bins (int) – Number of x bins.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • color (str) – Line and marker color.

  • template (str) – Plotly layout template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to go.Scatter.

Returns:

Interactive binned mean plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y is not numeric.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.binned_mean_plot_interactive(age, income) `

Notes

Empty bins are omitted from the plotted line.

dataviz.bivariate.binned_mean_plot_static(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 10, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str = 'steelblue', marker: str = 'o', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

  • x (SeriesLike) – Numeric values used to create bins.
