  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • color (str) – Line and marker color.

  • marker (str) – Marker style.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.plot.

Returns:

Binned mean plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y is not numeric.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.binned_mean_plot_static(age, income) `

Notes

Binned means help reveal nonlinear relationships in noisy scatter data.

dataviz.bivariate.bivariate_histogram(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, cmap: str = 'viridis', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static rectangular two-dimensional histogram.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • bins (int) – Number of bins in each direction.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • cmap (str) – Matplotlib colormap.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.hist2d.

Returns:

Two-dimensional histogram axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y is not numeric.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.bivariate_histogram_static(x, y) `

Notes

This is the rectangular-bin companion to a hexbin plot.

dataviz.bivariate.bivariate_histogram_interactive(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, nbinsx: int = 30, nbinsy: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, colorscale: str = 'Viridis', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive rectangular two-dimensional histogram.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • nbinsx (int) – Number of x-axis bins.

  • nbinsy (int) – Number of y-axis bins.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • colorscale (str) – Plotly colorscale.

  • template (str) – Plotly layout template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to go.Histogram2d.

Returns:

Interactive two-dimensional histogram.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y is not numeric.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.bivariate_histogram_interactive(x, y) `

Notes

This function is useful for dense data where individual markers are hard to read.

dataviz.bivariate.bivariate_histogram_static(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, cmap: str = 'viridis', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static rectangular two-dimensional histogram.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • bins (int) – Number of bins in each direction.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • cmap (str) – Matplotlib colormap.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.hist2d.

Returns:

Two-dimensional histogram axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y is not numeric.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.bivariate_histogram_static(x, y) `

Notes

This is the rectangular-bin companion to a hexbin plot.

dataviz.bivariate.bivariate_summary(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → BivariateStats[source]

Compute a compact statistical summary for two numeric variables.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or dataframe column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior: "drop", "raise", or "keep".

Returns:

Immutable summary with correlations, covariance, fit coefficients, and descriptive statistics.

Return type:

BivariateStats

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If fewer than two observations are available.

Example

`python stats = dv.bivariate_summary("height", "weight", data=df) `

Notes

Spearman correlation is computed with pandas ranking, so no SciPy dependency is required.

dataviz.bivariate.bland_altman(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, title: str = 'Bland-Altman Plot', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Axes

Create a static Bland-Altman agreement plot for two measurements.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – First measurement series or column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Second measurement series or column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Bland-Altman plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If fewer than two observations are available.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.bland_altman_static(method_a, method_b) `

Notes

Agreement limits are mean difference plus or minus 1.96 standard deviations.

dataviz.bivariate.bland_altman_interactive(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, title: str = 'Bland-Altman Plot', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Figure[source]

Create an interactive Bland-Altman agreement plot for two measurements.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – First measurement series or column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Second measurement series or column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Interactive Bland-Altman plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If fewer than two observations are available.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.bland_altman_interactive(method_a, method_b) `

Notes

Agreement limits are drawn as horizontal reference lines.

dataviz.bivariate.bland_altman_static(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, title: str = 'Bland-Altman Plot', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Axes[source]

Create a static Bland-Altman agreement plot for two measurements.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – First measurement series or column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Second measurement series or column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Bland-Altman plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If fewer than two observations are available.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.bland_altman_static(method_a, method_b) `

Notes

Agreement limits are mean difference plus or minus 1.96 standard deviations.

dataviz.bivariate.box_by_category(category: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, values: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static box plot of numeric values grouped by category.

Parameters:

  • category (SeriesLike) – Categorical group labels.

  • values (SeriesLike) – Numeric values to summarize.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.boxplot.

Returns:

Grouped box plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values are not numeric.

  • ValueError – If category and values lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.box_by_category_static(groups, scores) `

Notes

Categories are ordered by their natural pandas group order.

dataviz.bivariate.box_by_category_interactive(category: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, values: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive box plot of numeric values grouped by category.

Parameters:

  • category (SeriesLike) – Categorical group labels.

  • values (SeriesLike) – Numeric values to summarize.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • template (str) – Plotly layout template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to go.Box.

Returns:

Interactive grouped box plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values are not numeric.

  • ValueError – If category and values lengths differ.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.box_by_category_interactive(groups, scores) `

Notes

Plotly handles per-category grouping from the x values.

dataviz.bivariate.box_by_category_static(category: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, values: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static box plot of numeric values grouped by category.

Parameters:

  • category (SeriesLike) – Categorical group labels.

  • values (SeriesLike) – Numeric values to summarize.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.boxplot.

Returns:

Grouped box plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values are not numeric.

  • ValueError – If category and values lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.box_by_category_static(groups, scores) `

Notes

Categories are ordered by their natural pandas group order.

dataviz.bivariate.bubble_plot(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, size: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, color: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series | None = None, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, size_scale: float = 300.0, alpha: float = 0.6, cmap: str = 'viridis', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static bubble plot for two variables and a size dimension.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • size (ArrayLike) – Numeric values mapped to marker area.

  • color (Optional[ArrayLike]) – Optional numeric values mapped to marker color.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label. Defaults to the x series name or "X".

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label. Defaults to the y series name or "Y".

  • size_scale (float) – Multiplier used to scale marker area.

  • alpha (float) – Marker opacity.

  • cmap (str) – Matplotlib colormap for numeric colors.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.scatter.

Returns:

Bubble plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be interpreted as numeric arrays.

  • ValueError – If x, y, and size lengths do not match.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.bubble_plot_static(x, y, population) `

Notes

Use bubble_plot_interactive for a Plotly figure with hover support.

dataviz.bivariate.bubble_plot_interactive(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, size: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, color: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series | None = None, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, size_scale: float = 40.0, opacity: float = 0.7, colorscale: str = 'Viridis', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive bubble plot for two variables and a size dimension.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • size (ArrayLike) – Numeric values mapped to marker size.

  • color (Optional[ArrayLike]) – Optional numeric values mapped to marker color.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label. Defaults to the x series name or "X".

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label. Defaults to the y series name or "Y".

  • size_scale (float) – Maximum marker size.

  • opacity (float) – Marker opacity.

  • colorscale (str) – Plotly colorscale for numeric colors.

  • template (str) – Plotly layout template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to go.Scatter.

Returns:

Interactive bubble plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be interpreted as numeric arrays.

  • ValueError – If x, y, and size lengths do not match.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.bubble_plot_interactive(x, y, population) `

Notes

Marker colors accept either a single color or one value per observation.

dataviz.bivariate.bubble_plot_static(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, size: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series, color: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series | None = None, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, size_scale: float = 300.0, alpha: float = 0.6, cmap: str = 'viridis', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static bubble plot for two variables and a size dimension.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • size (ArrayLike) – Numeric values mapped to marker area.

  • color (Optional[ArrayLike]) – Optional numeric values mapped to marker color.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label. Defaults to the x series name or "X".

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label. Defaults to the y series name or "Y".

  • size_scale (float) – Multiplier used to scale marker area.

  • alpha (float) – Marker opacity.

  • cmap (str) – Matplotlib colormap for numeric colors.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.scatter.

Returns:

Bubble plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be interpreted as numeric arrays.

  • ValueError – If x, y, and size lengths do not match.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.bubble_plot_static(x, y, population) `

Notes

Use bubble_plot_interactive for a Plotly figure with hover support.

dataviz.bivariate.conditional_box(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, bins: int = 10, title: str = 'Conditional Box Plot', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (12, 6), theme: str = 'default', na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Axes

Create a static box plot of y distributions across x bins.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Numeric conditioning variable or column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Numeric response variable or column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • bins (int) – Number of x bins.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Conditional box plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If bins are invalid.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.conditional_box_static(x, y, bins=6) `

Notes

This chart shows how the distribution of y changes over ranges of x.

dataviz.bivariate.conditional_box_interactive(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, bins: int = 10, title: str = 'Conditional Box Plot', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Figure[source]

Create an interactive box plot of y distributions across x bins.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Numeric conditioning variable or column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Numeric response variable or column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • bins (int) – Number of x bins.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Interactive conditional box plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If bins are invalid.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.conditional_box_interactive(x, y, bins=6) `

Notes

Each x bin is rendered as a separate box trace.

dataviz.bivariate.conditional_box_static(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, bins: int = 10, title: str = 'Conditional Box Plot', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (12, 6), theme: str = 'default', na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Axes[source]

Create a static box plot of y distributions across x bins.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Numeric conditioning variable or column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Numeric response variable or column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • bins (int) – Number of x bins.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Conditional box plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If bins are invalid.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.conditional_box_static(x, y, bins=6) `

Notes

This chart shows how the distribution of y changes over ranges of x.

dataviz.bivariate.correlation_heatmap(df: DataFrame, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 8), cmap: str = 'coolwarm', annot: bool = True, fmt: str = '.2f', cbar: bool = True, vmin: float | None = -1.0, vmax: float | None = 1.0, linewidths: float = 0.5, linecolor: str = 'gray', theme: str = 'default', font_size: int = 10, title_size: int = 14, label_size: int = 11, dpi: int = 100, style: str = 'default', columns: Iterable[str] | None = None, method: Literal['pearson', 'kendall', 'spearman'] = 'pearson', min_periods: int = 1, mask_upper: bool = False, absolute: bool = False, ax: Axes | None = None, return_corr: bool = False, **kwargs) → Axes | Tuple[Axes, DataFrame]

Create a static heatmap of pairwise correlations between dataframe columns.

Parameters:

  • df (pd.DataFrame) – Input dataframe containing variables to correlate.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size in inches.

  • cmap (str) – Matplotlib colormap.

  • annot (bool) – Whether to annotate cells with correlation values.

  • fmt (str) – Format string used for annotations.

  • cbar (bool) – Whether to draw a colorbar.

  • vmin (Optional[float]) – Minimum color scale value.

  • vmax (Optional[float]) – Maximum color scale value.

  • linewidths (float) – Grid line width between heatmap cells.

  • linecolor (str) – Grid line color.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • font_size (int) – Annotation and colorbar font size.

  • title_size (int) – Title font size.

  • label_size (int) – Axis-label font size.

  • dpi (int) – Figure DPI when a new figure is created.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • columns (Optional[Iterable[str]]) – Optional column subset to include.

  • method (CorrelationMethod) – Correlation method: "pearson", "kendall", or "spearman".

  • min_periods (int) – Minimum observations required per column pair.

  • mask_upper (bool) – Whether to hide the upper triangle.

  • absolute (bool) – Whether to plot absolute correlation values.

  • ax (Optional[MatplotlibAxes]) – Existing axes to draw into.

  • return_corr (bool) – Whether to return the computed matrix with the axes.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.imshow.

Returns:

Heatmap axes, optionally with correlation matrix.

Return type:

Union[matplotlib.axes.Axes, Tuple[matplotlib.axes.Axes, pandas.DataFrame]]

Raises:

  • TypeError – If df is not a dataframe.

  • ValueError – If columns are missing, too few numeric columns exist, or options are invalid.

Example

`python ax, corr = dv.correlation_heatmap_static(df, method="spearman", return_corr=True) `

Notes

Non-numeric columns are ignored after any explicit column selection.

dataviz.bivariate.correlation_heatmap_interactive(df: DataFrame, title: str | None = None, colorscale: str = 'RdBu', showscale: bool = True, annot: bool = True, hovermode: str = 'closest', template: str = 'plotly', font_size: int = 12, title_size: int = 16, height: int = 700, width: int = 800, columns: Iterable[str] | None = None, method: Literal['pearson', 'kendall', 'spearman'] = 'pearson', min_periods: int = 1, mask_upper: bool = False, absolute: bool = False, return_corr: bool = False, **kwargs) → Figure | Tuple[Figure, DataFrame][source]

Create an interactive heatmap of pairwise correlations between dataframe columns.

Parameters:

  • df (pd.DataFrame) – Input dataframe containing variables to correlate.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • colorscale (str) – Plotly colorscale.

  • showscale (bool) – Whether to show the color scale.

  • annot (bool) – Whether to annotate cells with correlation values.

  • hovermode (str) – Plotly hover mode.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • font_size (int) – Base font size.

  • title_size (int) – Title font size.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • columns (Optional[Iterable[str]]) – Optional column subset to include.

  • method (CorrelationMethod) – Correlation method: "pearson", "kendall", or "spearman".

  • min_periods (int) – Minimum observations required per column pair.

  • mask_upper (bool) – Whether to hide the upper triangle.

  • absolute (bool) – Whether to plot absolute correlation values.

  • return_corr (bool) – Whether to return the computed matrix with the figure.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to go.Heatmap.

Returns:

Heatmap figure, optionally with correlation matrix.

Return type:

Union[plotly.graph_objects.Figure, Tuple[plotly.graph_objects.Figure, pandas.DataFrame]]

Raises:

  • TypeError – If df is not a dataframe.

  • ValueError – If columns are missing, too few numeric columns exist, or options are invalid.

Example

`python fig = dv.correlation_heatmap_interactive(df, columns=["a", "b", "c"]) `

Notes

Masked cells are represented as missing values in the heatmap.

dataviz.bivariate.correlation_heatmap_static(df: DataFrame, title: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 8), cmap: str = 'coolwarm', annot: bool = True, fmt: str = '.2f', cbar: bool = True, vmin: float | None = -1.0, vmax: float | None = 1.0, linewidths: float = 0.5, linecolor: str = 'gray', theme: str = 'default', font_size: int = 10, title_size: int = 14, label_size: int = 11, dpi: int = 100, style: str = 'default', columns: Iterable[str] | None = None, method: Literal['pearson', 'kendall', 'spearman'] = 'pearson', min_periods: int = 1, mask_upper: bool = False, absolute: bool = False, ax: Axes | None = None, return_corr: bool = False, **kwargs) → Axes | Tuple[Axes, DataFrame][source]

Create a static heatmap of pairwise correlations between dataframe columns.

Parameters:

  • df (pd.DataFrame) – Input dataframe containing variables to correlate.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size in inches.

  • cmap (str) – Matplotlib colormap.

  • annot (bool) – Whether to annotate cells with correlation values.

  • fmt (str) – Format string used for annotations.

  • cbar (bool) – Whether to draw a colorbar.

  • vmin (Optional[float]) – Minimum color scale value.

  • vmax (Optional[float]) – Maximum color scale value.

  • linewidths (float) – Grid line width between heatmap cells.

  • linecolor (str) – Grid line color.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • font_size (int) – Annotation and colorbar font size.

  • title_size (int) – Title font size.

  • label_size (int) – Axis-label font size.

  • dpi (int) – Figure DPI when a new figure is created.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • columns (Optional[Iterable[str]]) – Optional column subset to include.

  • method (CorrelationMethod) – Correlation method: "pearson", "kendall", or "spearman".

  • min_periods (int) – Minimum observations required per column pair.

  • mask_upper (bool) – Whether to hide the upper triangle.

  • absolute (bool) – Whether to plot absolute correlation values.

  • ax (Optional[MatplotlibAxes]) – Existing axes to draw into.

  • return_corr (bool) – Whether to return the computed matrix with the axes.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.imshow.

Returns:

Heatmap axes, optionally with correlation matrix.

Return type:

Union[matplotlib.axes.Axes, Tuple[matplotlib.axes.Axes, pandas.DataFrame]]

Raises:

  • TypeError – If df is not a dataframe.

  • ValueError – If columns are missing, too few numeric columns exist, or options are invalid.

Example

`python ax, corr = dv.correlation_heatmap_static(df, method="spearman", return_corr=True) `

Notes

Non-numeric columns are ignored after any explicit column selection.

dataviz.bivariate.crosstab_heatmap(row_category: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, column_category: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, normalize: str | None = None, title: str = 'Crosstab Heatmap', cmap: str = 'Blues', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static heatmap for two categorical variables.

Parameters:

  • row_category (SeriesLike) – Categories shown on the y-axis.

  • column_category (SeriesLike) – Categories shown on the x-axis.

  • normalize (Optional[str]) – Normalization mode accepted by pandas.crosstab.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • cmap (str) – Matplotlib colormap.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.imshow.

Returns:

Crosstab heatmap axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If categories cannot be tabulated.

  • ValueError – If category lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.crosstab_heatmap_static(region, segment) `

Notes

Set normalize="index" or normalize="columns" for proportions.

dataviz.bivariate.crosstab_heatmap_interactive(row_category: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, column_category: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, normalize: str | None = None, title: str = 'Crosstab Heatmap', colorscale: str = 'Blues', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive heatmap for two categorical variables.

Parameters:

  • row_category (SeriesLike) – Categories shown on the y-axis.

  • column_category (SeriesLike) – Categories shown on the x-axis.

  • normalize (Optional[str]) – Normalization mode accepted by pandas.crosstab.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • colorscale (str) – Plotly colorscale.

  • template (str) – Plotly layout template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to go.Heatmap.

Returns:

Interactive crosstab heatmap.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If categories cannot be tabulated.

  • ValueError – If category lengths differ.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.crosstab_heatmap_interactive(region, segment) `

Notes

Hover labels expose the tabulated count or proportion.

dataviz.bivariate.crosstab_heatmap_static(row_category: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, column_category: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, normalize: str | None = None, title: str = 'Crosstab Heatmap', cmap: str = 'Blues', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static heatmap for two categorical variables.

Parameters:

  • row_category (SeriesLike) – Categories shown on the y-axis.

  • column_category (SeriesLike) – Categories shown on the x-axis.

  • normalize (Optional[str]) – Normalization mode accepted by pandas.crosstab.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • cmap (str) – Matplotlib colormap.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.imshow.

Returns:

Crosstab heatmap axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If categories cannot be tabulated.

  • ValueError – If category lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.crosstab_heatmap_static(region, segment) `

Notes

Set normalize="index" or normalize="columns" for proportions.

dataviz.bivariate.density_contour(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, levels: int = 8, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, cmap: str = 'viridis', fill: bool = False, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default') → Axes

Create a static contour plot from a two-dimensional histogram.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • bins (int) – Number of bins used to estimate density.

  • levels (int) – Number of contour levels.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • cmap (str) – Matplotlib colormap.

  • fill (bool) – Whether to draw filled contours.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

Returns:

Density contour axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y is not numeric.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.density_contour_static(x, y, fill=True) `

Notes

Density is estimated with numpy.histogram2d.

dataviz.bivariate.density_contour_interactive(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, colorscale: str = 'Viridis', contours_coloring: Literal['fill', 'heatmap', 'lines', 'none'] = 'fill', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive density contour plot.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • colorscale (str) – Plotly colorscale.

  • contours_coloring (Literal["fill", "heatmap", "lines", "none"]) – Contour coloring mode.

  • template (str) – Plotly layout template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to go.Histogram2dContour.

Returns:

Interactive density contour figure.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y is not numeric.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.density_contour_interactive(x, y) `

Notes

Plotly estimates the two-dimensional density from the supplied points.

dataviz.bivariate.density_contour_static(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, levels: int = 8, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, cmap: str = 'viridis', fill: bool = False, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default') → Axes[source]

Create a static contour plot from a two-dimensional histogram.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • bins (int) – Number of bins used to estimate density.

  • levels (int) – Number of contour levels.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • cmap (str) – Matplotlib colormap.

  • fill (bool) – Whether to draw filled contours.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

Returns:

Density contour axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y is not numeric.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.density_contour_static(x, y, fill=True) `

Notes

Density is estimated with numpy.histogram2d.

dataviz.bivariate.errorbar_plot(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, yerr: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series | None = None, xerr: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series | None = None, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str = 'steelblue', marker: str = 'o', capsize: float = 4.0, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static bivariate error-bar plot.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • yerr (Optional[ArrayLike]) – Symmetric or asymmetric y-error values.

  • xerr (Optional[ArrayLike]) – Symmetric or asymmetric x-error values.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • color (str) – Marker and line color.

  • marker (str) – Marker style.

  • capsize (float) – Error-bar cap size.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.errorbar.

Returns:

Error-bar plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If error values are incompatible with matplotlib.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.errorbar_plot_static(x, mean, yerr=std) `

Notes

Use this chart for estimates with uncertainty intervals.

dataviz.bivariate.errorbar_plot_interactive(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, yerr: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series | None = None, xerr: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series | None = None, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str = 'steelblue', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive bivariate error-bar plot.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • yerr (Optional[ArrayLike]) – Symmetric y-error values.

  • xerr (Optional[ArrayLike]) – Symmetric x-error values.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • color (str) – Marker and line color.

  • template (str) – Plotly layout template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to go.Scatter.

Returns:

Interactive error-bar plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If error values are incompatible with Plotly.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.errorbar_plot_interactive(x, mean, yerr=std) `

Notes

Plotly supports symmetric error arrays through array values.

dataviz.bivariate.errorbar_plot_static(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, yerr: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series | None = None, xerr: Sequence[float] | ndarray | Series | None = None, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str = 'steelblue', marker: str = 'o', capsize: float = 4.0, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static bivariate error-bar plot.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • yerr (Optional[ArrayLike]) – Symmetric or asymmetric y-error values.

  • xerr (Optional[ArrayLike]) – Symmetric or asymmetric x-error values.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • color (str) – Marker and line color.

  • marker (str) – Marker style.

  • capsize (float) – Error-bar cap size.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.errorbar.

Returns:

Error-bar plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If error values are incompatible with matplotlib.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.errorbar_plot_static(x, mean, yerr=std) `

Notes

Use this chart for estimates with uncertainty intervals.

dataviz.bivariate.grouped_bar(category: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, values: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, aggfunc: str | Callable[[Series], float] = 'mean', title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str = 'steelblue', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static aggregated bar chart for a numeric value by category.

Parameters:

  • category (SeriesLike) – Categorical group labels.

  • values (SeriesLike) – Numeric values aggregated within each category.

  • aggfunc (Aggregator) – Pandas aggregation function or name.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • color (str) – Bar color.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.bar.

Returns:

Aggregated bar chart axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be aggregated.

  • ValueError – If category and values lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.grouped_bar_static(groups, sales, aggfunc="sum") `

Notes

Use box or violin plots when the distribution inside each group matters.

dataviz.bivariate.grouped_bar_interactive(category: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, values: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, aggfunc: str | Callable[[Series], float] = 'mean', title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str = 'steelblue', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive aggregated bar chart for a numeric value by category.

Parameters:

  • category (SeriesLike) – Categorical group labels.

  • values (SeriesLike) – Numeric values aggregated within each category.

  • aggfunc (Aggregator) – Pandas aggregation function or name.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • color (str) – Bar color.

  • template (str) – Plotly layout template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to go.Bar.

Returns:

Interactive aggregated bar chart.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be aggregated.

  • ValueError – If category and values lengths differ.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.grouped_bar_interactive(groups, sales, aggfunc="median") `

Notes

Aggregation is performed with pandas before plotting.

dataviz.bivariate.grouped_bar_static(category: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, values: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, aggfunc: str | Callable[[Series], float] = 'mean', title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str = 'steelblue', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static aggregated bar chart for a numeric value by category.

Parameters:

  • category (SeriesLike) – Categorical group labels.

  • values (SeriesLike) – Numeric values aggregated within each category.

  • aggfunc (Aggregator) – Pandas aggregation function or name.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • color (str) – Bar color.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.bar.

Returns:

Aggregated bar chart axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If values cannot be aggregated.

  • ValueError – If category and values lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.grouped_bar_static(groups, sales, aggfunc="sum") `

Notes

Use box or violin plots when the distribution inside each group matters.

dataviz.bivariate.hexbin_plot(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, gridsize: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, cmap: str = 'viridis', mincnt: int | None = 1, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static hexbin density plot for dense bivariate data.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • gridsize (int) – Number of hexagons in the x direction.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • cmap (str) – Matplotlib colormap for hexagon counts.

  • mincnt (Optional[int]) – Minimum observations required to draw a hexagon.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.hexbin.

Returns:

Hexbin plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y is not numeric.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.hexbin_plot_static(x, y, gridsize=40) `

Notes

Hexbin charts are useful when scatter points overlap heavily.

dataviz.bivariate.hexbin_plot_interactive(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, nbinsx: int = 30, nbinsy: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, colorscale: str = 'Viridis', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive bivariate histogram density plot.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • nbinsx (int) – Number of bins along the x-axis.

  • nbinsy (int) – Number of bins along the y-axis.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • colorscale (str) – Plotly colorscale for bin counts.

  • template (str) – Plotly layout template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to go.Histogram2d.

Returns:

Interactive bivariate density figure.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y is not numeric.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.hexbin_plot_interactive(x, y) `

Notes

Plotly does not render true hexagons here; it uses rectangular 2D bins.

dataviz.bivariate.hexbin_plot_static(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, gridsize: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, cmap: str = 'viridis', mincnt: int | None = 1, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', style: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static hexbin density plot for dense bivariate data.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • gridsize (int) – Number of hexagons in the x direction.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • cmap (str) – Matplotlib colormap for hexagon counts.

  • mincnt (Optional[int]) – Minimum observations required to draw a hexagon.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the axes.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.hexbin.

Returns:

Hexbin plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y is not numeric.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.hexbin_plot_static(x, y, gridsize=40) `

Notes

Hexbin charts are useful when scatter points overlap heavily.

dataviz.bivariate.joint_scatter_hist(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, scatter_color: str = 'steelblue', hist_color: str = 'gray', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (8, 8), theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static joint scatter plot with marginal histograms.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • bins (int) – Number of bins used in marginal histograms.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • scatter_color (str) – Scatter marker color.

  • hist_color (str) – Histogram bar color.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the main axes.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.scatter.

Returns:

Main scatter axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y is incompatible with matplotlib.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.joint_scatter_hist_static(x, y) `

Notes

The returned axes is the main scatter panel; marginal axes are attached to the same figure.

dataviz.bivariate.joint_scatter_hist_interactive(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, scatter_color: str = 'steelblue', hist_color: str = 'gray', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 800, width: int = 800, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive joint scatter plot with marginal histograms.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • bins (int) – Number of bins used in marginal histograms.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • scatter_color (str) – Scatter marker color.

  • hist_color (str) – Histogram bar color.

  • template (str) – Plotly layout template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to the scatter trace.

Returns:

Interactive joint distribution figure.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y is incompatible with Plotly.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.joint_scatter_hist_interactive(x, y) `

Notes

Marginal histograms share axes with the central scatter plot.

dataviz.bivariate.joint_scatter_hist_static(x: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, bins: int = 30, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, scatter_color: str = 'steelblue', hist_color: str = 'gray', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (8, 8), theme: str = 'default', **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static joint scatter plot with marginal histograms.

Parameters:

  • x (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the x-axis.

  • y (SeriesLike) – Values plotted along the y-axis.

  • bins (int) – Number of bins used in marginal histograms.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • scatter_color (str) – Scatter marker color.

  • hist_color (str) – Histogram bar color.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Theme name applied to the main axes.

  • **kwargs – Additional arguments forwarded to Axes.scatter.

Returns:

Main scatter axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If x or y is incompatible with matplotlib.

  • ValueError – If x and y lengths differ.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.joint_scatter_hist_static(x, y) `

Notes

The returned axes is the main scatter panel; marginal axes are attached to the same figure.

dataviz.bivariate.lag_plot(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, lag: int = 1, title: str = 'Lag Relationship Plot', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Axes

Create a static lagged relationship plot for two ordered variables.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Leading series or column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Lagged series or column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • lag (int) – Positive lag applied to y.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Lag relationship axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If lag is invalid for the available observations.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.lag_plot_static(series_a, series_b, lag=3) `

Notes

The plot compares x[t] against y[t-lag].

dataviz.bivariate.lag_plot_interactive(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, lag: int = 1, title: str = 'Lag Relationship Plot', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Figure[source]

Create an interactive lagged relationship plot for two ordered variables.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Leading series or column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Lagged series or column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • lag (int) – Positive lag applied to y.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Interactive lag relationship plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If lag is invalid for the available observations.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.lag_plot_interactive(series_a, series_b, lag=3) `

Notes

Lag plots are useful for ordered or time-indexed data.

dataviz.bivariate.lag_plot_static(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, lag: int = 1, title: str = 'Lag Relationship Plot', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Axes[source]

Create a static lagged relationship plot for two ordered variables.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Leading series or column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Lagged series or column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • lag (int) – Positive lag applied to y.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Lag relationship axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If lag is invalid for the available observations.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.lag_plot_static(series_a, series_b, lag=3) `

Notes

The plot compares x[t] against y[t-lag].

dataviz.bivariate.line_plot(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, linewidth: float = 2.0, linestyle: str = '-', marker: str | None = None, markersize: int = 6, alpha: float = 1.0, grid: bool = True, grid_alpha: float = 0.3, theme: str = 'default', font_size: int = 10, title_size: int = 14, label_size: int = 11, dpi: int = 100, style: str = 'default', ax: Axes | None = None, na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop', sort_x: bool = False, rolling_window: int | None = None, rolling_color: str = 'crimson', fill_to: float | None = None, fill_alpha: float = 0.2, hline: float | None = None, vline: float | None = None, **kwargs) → Axes

Create a static line plot that shows ordered or time-based changes.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or a column name when data is provided.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or a column name when data is provided.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe used for column lookup.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size in inches.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Line color.

  • linewidth (float) – Line width.

  • linestyle (str) – Line style.

  • marker (Optional[str]) – Marker style.

  • markersize (int) – Marker size.

  • alpha (float) – Line opacity from 0 to 1.

  • grid (bool) – Whether to show grid lines.

  • grid_alpha (float) – Grid opacity.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • font_size (int) – Tick and legend font size.

  • title_size (int) – Title font size.

  • label_size (int) – Axis-label font size.

  • dpi (int) – Figure DPI when a new figure is created.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • ax (Optional[MatplotlibAxes]) – Existing axes to draw into.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior: "drop", "raise", or "keep".

  • sort_x (bool) – Whether to sort paired observations by x before drawing.

  • rolling_window (Optional[int]) – Optional rolling mean window for y values.

  • rolling_color (str) – Rolling mean line color.

  • fill_to (Optional[float]) – Optional baseline for filled area under the line.

  • fill_alpha (float) – Area fill opacity.

  • hline (Optional[float]) – Optional horizontal reference line.

  • vline (Optional[float]) – Optional vertical reference line.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.plot.

Returns:

Configured matplotlib axes containing the line plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved or plotted.

  • ValueError – If input lengths differ, inputs are empty, or options are invalid.

Example

`python ax = dv.line_plot_static("date", "sales", data=df, rolling_window=7) `

Notes

sort_x=True is helpful when observations are not already ordered.

dataviz.bivariate.line_plot_interactive(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, color: str | None = None, marker_color: str | None = None, linewidth: float = 2.0, mode: str = 'lines', marker_size: int = 8, showlegend: bool = True, hovermode: str = 'x unified', template: str = 'plotly', font_size: int = 12, title_size: int = 16, height: int = 600, width: int = 1000, na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop', sort_x: bool = False, rolling_window: int | None = None, rolling_color: str = 'crimson', fill_to: float | None = None, hline: float | None = None, vline: float | None = None, **kwargs) → Figure[source]

Create an interactive line plot that shows ordered or time-based changes.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or a column name when data is provided.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or a column name when data is provided.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe used for column lookup.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Line color.

  • marker_color (Optional[str]) – Alias for color.

  • linewidth (float) – Line width.

  • mode (str) – Plotly scatter mode.

  • marker_size (int) – Marker size.

  • showlegend (bool) – Whether to show the legend.

  • hovermode (str) – Plotly hover mode.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • font_size (int) – Base font size.

  • title_size (int) – Title font size.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior: "drop", "raise", or "keep".

  • sort_x (bool) – Whether to sort paired observations by x before drawing.

  • rolling_window (Optional[int]) – Optional rolling mean window for y values.

  • rolling_color (str) – Rolling mean line color.

  • fill_to (Optional[float]) – Optional baseline for filled area under the line.

  • hline (Optional[float]) – Optional horizontal reference line.

  • vline (Optional[float]) – Optional vertical reference line.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to go.Scatter.

Returns:

Configured Plotly line figure.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved or plotted.

  • ValueError – If input lengths differ, inputs are empty, or options are invalid.

Example

`python fig = dv.line_plot_interactive("date", "sales", data=df, sort_x=True) `

Notes

Area fill is implemented with Plotly’s tozeroy mode when fill_to is zero.

dataviz.bivariate.line_plot_static(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, title: str | None = None, xlabel: str | None = None, ylabel: str | None = None, figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), color: str | None = None, linewidth: float = 2.0, linestyle: str = '-', marker: str | None = None, markersize: int = 6, alpha: float = 1.0, grid: bool = True, grid_alpha: float = 0.3, theme: str = 'default', font_size: int = 10, title_size: int = 14, label_size: int = 11, dpi: int = 100, style: str = 'default', ax: Axes | None = None, na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop', sort_x: bool = False, rolling_window: int | None = None, rolling_color: str = 'crimson', fill_to: float | None = None, fill_alpha: float = 0.2, hline: float | None = None, vline: float | None = None, **kwargs) → Axes[source]

Create a static line plot that shows ordered or time-based changes.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or a column name when data is provided.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or a column name when data is provided.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe used for column lookup.

  • title (Optional[str]) – Chart title. Defaults to a generated title.

  • xlabel (Optional[str]) – X-axis label.

  • ylabel (Optional[str]) – Y-axis label.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Matplotlib figure size in inches.

  • color (Optional[str]) – Line color.

  • linewidth (float) – Line width.

  • linestyle (str) – Line style.

  • marker (Optional[str]) – Marker style.

  • markersize (int) – Marker size.

  • alpha (float) – Line opacity from 0 to 1.

  • grid (bool) – Whether to show grid lines.

  • grid_alpha (float) – Grid opacity.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • font_size (int) – Tick and legend font size.

  • title_size (int) – Title font size.

  • label_size (int) – Axis-label font size.

  • dpi (int) – Figure DPI when a new figure is created.

  • style (str) – Matplotlib style context.

  • ax (Optional[MatplotlibAxes]) – Existing axes to draw into.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior: "drop", "raise", or "keep".

  • sort_x (bool) – Whether to sort paired observations by x before drawing.

  • rolling_window (Optional[int]) – Optional rolling mean window for y values.

  • rolling_color (str) – Rolling mean line color.

  • fill_to (Optional[float]) – Optional baseline for filled area under the line.

  • fill_alpha (float) – Area fill opacity.

  • hline (Optional[float]) – Optional horizontal reference line.

  • vline (Optional[float]) – Optional vertical reference line.

  • **kwargs – Additional keyword arguments forwarded to Axes.plot.

Returns:

Configured matplotlib axes containing the line plot.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved or plotted.

  • ValueError – If input lengths differ, inputs are empty, or options are invalid.

Example

`python ax = dv.line_plot_static("date", "sales", data=df, rolling_window=7) `

Notes

sort_x=True is helpful when observations are not already ordered.

dataviz.bivariate.outlier_scatter(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, method: Literal['zscore', 'iqr'] = 'iqr', threshold: float = 1.5, title: str = 'Outlier Scatter', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), normal_color: str = 'steelblue', outlier_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default', na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Axes

Create a static scatter plot highlighting bivariate outliers.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or dataframe column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • method (Literal["zscore", "iqr"]) – Outlier detection method.

  • threshold (float) – Z-score threshold or IQR multiplier.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • normal_color (str) – Color for non-outlier observations.

  • outlier_color (str) – Color for outlier observations.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Scatter plot axes with outliers highlighted.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If threshold or method is invalid.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.outlier_scatter_static(x, y, method="zscore", threshold=3) `

Notes

A point is highlighted when either its x or y value is considered an outlier.

dataviz.bivariate.outlier_scatter_interactive(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, method: Literal['zscore', 'iqr'] = 'iqr', threshold: float = 1.5, title: str = 'Outlier Scatter', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Figure[source]

Create an interactive scatter plot highlighting bivariate outliers.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or dataframe column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • method (Literal["zscore", "iqr"]) – Outlier detection method.

  • threshold (float) – Z-score threshold or IQR multiplier.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Interactive scatter plot with outliers highlighted.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If threshold or method is invalid.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.outlier_scatter_interactive(x, y) `

Notes

Outlier labels are computed from x and y independently.

dataviz.bivariate.outlier_scatter_static(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, method: Literal['zscore', 'iqr'] = 'iqr', threshold: float = 1.5, title: str = 'Outlier Scatter', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), normal_color: str = 'steelblue', outlier_color: str = 'crimson', theme: str = 'default', na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Axes[source]

Create a static scatter plot highlighting bivariate outliers.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or dataframe column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • method (Literal["zscore", "iqr"]) – Outlier detection method.

  • threshold (float) – Z-score threshold or IQR multiplier.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • normal_color (str) – Color for non-outlier observations.

  • outlier_color (str) – Color for outlier observations.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Scatter plot axes with outliers highlighted.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If threshold or method is invalid.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.outlier_scatter_static(x, y, method="zscore", threshold=3) `

Notes

A point is highlighted when either its x or y value is considered an outlier.

dataviz.bivariate.quantile_bin_plot(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, q: int = 10, statistic: Literal['mean', 'median'] = 'mean', title: str = 'Quantile Bin Plot', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Axes

Create a static plot of y summaries across x quantile bins.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or dataframe column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • q (int) – Number of quantile bins.

  • statistic (Literal["mean", "median"]) – Summary statistic to plot.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Quantile-bin summary plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If quantile bins or statistic are invalid.

Example

`python ax = dv.bivariate.quantile_bin_plot_static(x, y, q=5, statistic="median") `

Notes

Quantile bins keep group sizes roughly balanced.

dataviz.bivariate.quantile_bin_plot_interactive(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, q: int = 10, statistic: Literal['mean', 'median'] = 'mean', title: str = 'Quantile Bin Plot', template: str = 'plotly', height: int = 600, width: int = 900, na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Figure[source]

Create an interactive plot of y summaries across x quantile bins.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or dataframe column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • q (int) – Number of quantile bins.

  • statistic (Literal["mean", "median"]) – Summary statistic to plot.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • template (str) – Plotly template.

  • height (int) – Figure height in pixels.

  • width (int) – Figure width in pixels.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Interactive quantile-bin summary plot.

Return type:

plotly.graph_objects.Figure

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If quantile bins or statistic are invalid.

Example

`python fig = dv.bivariate.quantile_bin_plot_interactive(x, y) `

Notes

The hover labels use quantile-bin centers on the x-axis.

dataviz.bivariate.quantile_bin_plot_static(x: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, y: str | Series | Sequence[float] | ndarray, data: DataFrame | None = None, q: int = 10, statistic: Literal['mean', 'median'] = 'mean', title: str = 'Quantile Bin Plot', figsize: Tuple[int, int] = (10, 6), theme: str = 'default', na_policy: Literal['drop', 'raise', 'keep'] = 'drop') → Axes[source]

Create a static plot of y summaries across x quantile bins.

Parameters:

  • x (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – X values or dataframe column name.

  • y (Union[str, SeriesLike]) – Y values or dataframe column name.

  • data (Optional[pd.DataFrame]) – Optional dataframe for column lookup.

  • q (int) – Number of quantile bins.

  • statistic (Literal["mean", "median"]) – Summary statistic to plot.

  • title (str) – Chart title.

  • figsize (FigureSize) – Figure size in inches.

  • theme (str) – Named style theme.

  • na_policy (NaPolicy) – Missing-value behavior.

Returns:

Quantile-bin summary plot axes.

Return type:

matplotlib.axes.Axes

Raises:

  • TypeError – If inputs cannot be resolved as numeric series.

  • ValueError – If quantile bins or statistic are invalid.

Example
